data.q: What does ARIA stand for in web accessibility?
data.e: ARIA stands for Accessible Rich Internet Applications and helps improve accessibility for dynamic content.

data.q: Which ARIA attribute is used to indicate the role of an element?
data.e: The 'role' attribute defines the purpose of an element to assistive technologies.

data.q: What is the purpose of the aria-label attribute?
o: To provide a visible label for an element
To give an accessible name to an element for screen readers
To define the region of the page
data.e: aria-label provides an accessible name to elements that may not have visible labels.

data.q: Which attribute helps to describe the currently selected item's state in a listbox?
data.e: aria-selected indicates whether an element is selected in widgets like listboxes.

data.q: Why should you avoid using empty alt attributes on images that convey information?
o: Because screen readers will read the filename
Because empty alt attributes hide the image from screen readers
No reason, empty alt is always recommended
data.e: Empty alt attributes hide images from screen readers and should only be used for decorative images.

o: It defines an element as a live region where updates should be announced by screen readers
It locks an element from updates
It is used to define a clickable button
It specifies the language of text
data.e: aria-live marks areas of a page where dynamic content changes should be announced to users using assistive technology.

data.q: Which ARIA attribute improves screen reader announcements for expanding/collapsing sections?
data.e: aria-expanded indicates whether an expandable section is currently open or collapsed.

data.q: When should you use aria-hidden="true" on an element?
o: When the element should be hidden from all users
When the element should be hidden from screen readers but visible visually
To hide elements from sighted users only
To hide an element during printing
data.e: aria-hidden="true" hides content from assistive technologies but keeps it visible on the screen.

data.q: Which HTML element provides the best native accessibility for buttons without ARIA?
data.e: The <button> element natively supports keyboard accessibility and screen readers.

data.q: What is the best practice for making custom UI controls accessible?
o: Just add a click event handler
Use semantic HTML or add appropriate ARIA roles and properties
Make it a <span> and style it
data.e: Custom controls should use semantic HTML or ARIA roles/attributes and support keyboard navigation.

data.q: How can you specify the language of the page for screen readers and translation tools?
o: Using lang attribute on the <html> element
data.e: The lang attribute on the <html> tag informs screen readers and translation services about the page language.

data.q: Which ARIA property indicates the relationship between an element and a description elsewhere on the page?
data.e: aria-describedby points to the ID of an element that provides a description for the current element.

o: It determines whether changes in a live region should be presented as a whole or only the changed parts
data.e: aria-atomic controls whether assistive technologies present all of the live region's content or only changed parts.

data.q: Which role would you assign via ARIA to an element representing a dialog or popup?
data.e: The 'dialog' role defines a dialog or popup window for screen reader users.

data.q: True or False: When native HTML controls exist, it's recommended to use ARIA roles instead of the native elements.
data.e: Native HTML elements should be preferred over ARIA roles for accessibility unless no native element exists.
